\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage{times}
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
\usepackage{geometry}
\geometry{a4paper, margin=2.5cm}
\usepackage{lipsum}

\title{Deep Learning for Natural Language Processing:\\A Numbered-Reference Survey}

\author{First Author \\ Test University \\ \texttt{first@test.edu}}

\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
This paper surveys deep learning methods for natural language processing.
We cover foundational architectures and their applications.
References are in numbered IEEE style, which differs from ACL author-year format
and exercises different splitter and parser code paths.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
The transformer architecture [1] revolutionized NLP. Pre-trained models [2,3]
further advanced the field by enabling transfer learning at scale.
Large language models [4] demonstrated few-shot learning capabilities.

\lipsum[1-3]

\section{Background}
Word embeddings [5,6] provided dense vector representations.
Contextualized embeddings [7] improved on static representations by conditioning
on the full input sequence.

\lipsum[4-6]

\section{Methods}
We build on sequence-to-sequence pre-training [8] and extend it to our setting.
The tokenization approach follows [9]. Our evaluation uses standard benchmarks [10].

\lipsum[7-9]

\section{Experiments}
\lipsum[10-12]
Results are consistent with prior work [1,3,4].

\section{Related Work}
\lipsum[13-15]
See also [5,6,7,8,9,10] for foundational methods.

\section{Conclusion}
\lipsum[16]
